title: Scrambled Eggs with Mushrooms (24g Protein Breakfast) | Eati
introduction: Scrambled eggs with mushrooms is a 280-calorie, 24g-protein breakfast that you can cook in one pan in about 8 minutes. Earthy mushrooms add volume and umami depth without real calories, while 3 whole eggs deliver complete protein, choline, and fat-soluble vitamins. A go-to weekday breakfast that fits any deficit.

sections.heading: Ingredients
sections.content:
- 3 large eggs
- 1 cup (90g) sliced mushrooms (cremini or white button)
- 1 garlic clove, minced
- 1 tsp olive oil or butter
- 1 tbsp chopped chives or parsley
- Salt and black pepper
- Optional: 1 oz shredded cheese, splash of milk, chili flakes

sections.heading: Instructions
sections.content:
1. Heat 1/2 tsp oil in a nonstick skillet over medium-high heat.
2. Add sliced mushrooms and cook 4–5 minutes until golden brown and liquid has evaporated.
3. Add garlic; stir 30 seconds until fragrant.
4. Reduce heat to medium-low. Add remaining oil/butter.
5. Whisk eggs with a pinch of salt and pour into the pan.
6. Stir gently with a silicone spatula, folding until soft curds form (about 2 minutes).
7. Top with chives, optional cheese, and log in Eati.

sections.heading: Calories, Fat, and Protein (Estimated)
sections.content:
Calories: 280 kcal per serving (base recipe)
Fat: 16g
Protein: 24g
Carbs: 4g

With 1 oz cheese: 390 cal, 24g fat, 31g protein. Use 2 whole + 3 whites to drop to 215 cal with 26g protein.

sections.heading: Why Scrambled Eggs with Mushrooms Is a Smart Breakfast
sections.content:
This is the rare breakfast that's simple, cheap, and nutritionally excellent. Here's why it earns permanent rotation:

Macro breakdown:
• 280 calories
• 24g protein (complete, highest biological value)
• 16g fat (5g saturated, 10g mono/poly)
• 4g carbs
• ~8.5g protein per 100 calories — very good

Three whole eggs provide:
• 18g protein.
• ~150% daily choline (crucial for brain health).
• 100% DV vitamin D (from yolks).
• B12, selenium, riboflavin.
• Lutein + zeaxanthin for eye health.

Mushrooms are nutrition sleepers:
• 20 cal per cup, almost zero net carbs.
• 2g of protein per cup (actually surprisingly protein-dense for a vegetable).
• Beta-glucans support immune function.
• Ergothioneine: a unique antioxidant mostly found in mushrooms.
• Natural MSG compounds create deep umami flavor with no added salt.

Satiety and fat loss:
• Protein + fat combo keeps you full 3–4 hours.
• Very low carb — great for low-carb or keto approaches.
• High volume per calorie thanks to mushrooms.
• Low inflammation profile.

How it compares:
• McDonald's Egg McMuffin: 300 cal, 17g protein, 12g fat.
• Panera spinach + bacon egg sandwich: 440 cal, 23g protein.
• This recipe: 280 cal, 24g protein.

For more breakfast ideas, see [20 high-protein low-calorie breakfast ideas](/blog/20-high-protein-low-calorie-breakfast-ideas). For lower-cal versions, see [egg white omelette with spinach](/blog/egg-white-omelette-spinach-recipe).

sections.heading: How to Cook the Perfect Scrambled Eggs
sections.content:
Restaurant-quality scrambled eggs at home take 90 seconds to learn and make every breakfast noticeably better:

Three techniques ranked by creaminess:

1. Gordon Ramsay method (ultra-creamy):
• Crack eggs directly into cold nonstick pan with butter.
• Heat over medium-low, stirring constantly with silicone spatula.
• Move pan on and off heat every 30 seconds to prevent over-cooking.
• Remove from heat 30 seconds before eggs look fully set.
• Add 1 tbsp cold cream or sour cream at the end.
• Result: soft, small-curd, pudding-like texture.

2. French method (medium creaminess):
• Whisk eggs with 1 tsp water or milk.
• Melt butter in pan over medium-low.
• Pour eggs in and stir gently with wooden spoon in a figure-8 motion.
• Cook 3–4 minutes until soft curds form.
• Result: small-to-medium curds with consistent creaminess.

3. Diner-style (larger curds, faster):
• Whisk eggs well.
• Heat pan to medium-high with oil/butter.
• Pour eggs and let set 30 seconds before stirring in large folds.
• Cook 2–3 minutes total.
• Result: larger curds, firmer texture.

Universal rules:
• Low to medium heat is the rule — high heat = dry rubbery eggs.
• Remove eggs 30 seconds before they 'look' done — residual heat finishes them.
• Nonstick pan or well-seasoned cast iron only.
• Use a silicone or wooden spatula — metal breaks up curds too aggressively.
• Season at the end (salt too early draws out moisture and makes eggs watery).

Common scrambled egg mistakes:
• Stirring too much: creates small, tight curds.
• Cooking too long: dry, pale, rubbery texture.
• Adding milk: actually dilutes flavor and makes eggs watery (use cream sparingly instead).
• Using cold eggs straight from fridge: they cook unevenly. Let them sit 10 min.

sections.heading: Mushroom Guide: Types, Benefits, and Cooking Tips
sections.content:
Mushrooms are more than filler — they're one of the most underrated breakfast ingredients:

Best types for breakfast scrambles:

1. Cremini (aka baby bella):
• Earthy, meaty flavor.
• Best all-around for scrambles.
• $3–4/lb at most grocery stores.

2. White button:
• Mildest flavor.
• Cheapest ($2–3/lb).
• Great for kids or mushroom-cautious eaters.

3. Shiitake:
• Deeper umami, slightly chewy texture.
• $8–12/lb — splurge option.
• Adds an 'Asian breakfast' feel.

4. Oyster:
• Mild, delicate.
• $6–8/lb.
• Cook quickly — don't overdo.

5. Portobello:
• Meatiest.
• Great for brunch-style plates.
• Grill or pan-sear before scrambling.

Nutritional highlights per cup sliced:
• 20 calories, 2g protein, 2g fiber.
• 18% DV riboflavin (B2).
• 16% DV selenium.
• 12% DV niacin (B3).
• Trace vitamin D — boost by leaving mushrooms in sunlight for 30 min before cooking (seriously, it multiplies their vitamin D 10×).

Cooking mushrooms perfectly:
• Don't wash — wipe with damp paper towel. Water makes them soggy.
• Slice after cleaning, not before.
• Medium-high heat with minimal oil.
• Don't crowd the pan — too many at once steams instead of browns.
• Don't salt until golden brown — salt too early extracts water and prevents browning.
• Cook until liquid has evaporated and edges are golden — about 4–5 minutes.

Meal prep note:
Cooked mushrooms keep 3 days in fridge, freeze for 3 months. Sauté a whole container on Sunday and use throughout the week — also great for pasta, rice bowls, and omelettes.

sections.heading: 6 Scrambled Egg + Mushroom Variations
sections.content:
Customize based on your goals and mood. All variations stay around 25g protein:

1. Mediterranean (310 cal, 26g protein):
• Base + 1 oz feta + cherry tomatoes + oregano + black olives.

2. Truffle-inspired (320 cal, 26g protein):
• Base + 1 tsp truffle oil + 1 tbsp parmesan + fresh thyme.
• Turns a simple breakfast into a restaurant plate.

3. Asian-inspired (295 cal, 25g protein):
• Use shiitake instead of cremini + 1 tsp soy sauce + 1/2 tsp sesame oil + scallions. Pair with a side of kimchi.

4. Spanish-style (340 cal, 27g protein):
• Base + 2 oz chorizo (crumbled, cooked first) + smoked paprika + spinach.

5. Herb & goat cheese (330 cal, 27g protein):
• Base + 1 oz crumbled goat cheese + chives + dill + parsley.

6. Spicy Mexican (315 cal, 27g protein):
• Base + 1/4 diced jalapeño + salsa + cotija cheese + cilantro.

Plate pairings:
• +40 cal: 1 slice turkey bacon.
• +75 cal: 1/2 cup black beans.
• +100 cal: 1 slice whole-grain toast.
• +120 cal: 1/2 avocado slices.
• +50 cal: side salad with light vinaigrette.

Lower-calorie egg modifications:
• Use 1 whole egg + 4 egg whites: 190 cal, 28g protein. Inspired by our [egg white omelette with spinach](/blog/egg-white-omelette-spinach-recipe).
• Use 2 whole + 3 whites: 215 cal, 26g protein.
• Skip cheese and oil: 200 cal, 22g protein.

midArticleCta: Log 24g of protein in your first meal. Track these scrambled eggs in Eati and watch your calorie and protein targets take care of themselves.
conclusion: A simple 8-minute breakfast with 24g of protein and the best macro profile a scramble can offer. Keep a carton of eggs and a box of mushrooms on hand and you always have a filling, low-carb option ready to cook.
metaTitle: Scrambled Eggs with Mushrooms: 24g Protein, 280 Calories (Easy Breakfast)
metaDescription: Creamy scrambled eggs with mushrooms — 24g protein in 280 calories. Includes 6 variations, mushroom guide, and the perfect scrambled egg technique step by step.

faqs.question: How many calories in scrambled eggs with mushrooms?
faqs.answer: About 280 calories with 3 whole eggs, 1 cup sliced mushrooms, and 1 tsp olive oil. Delivers 24g protein and just 4g carbs. Adding 1 oz of cheese brings it to 390 cal with 31g protein.

faqs.question: Are scrambled eggs and mushrooms good for weight loss?
faqs.answer: Yes. Low carbs, high protein, and mushrooms add volume with minimal calories. The 280-calorie plate delivers 24g protein and keeps you full 3–4 hours, making it a reliable fat-loss breakfast.

faqs.question: How do you cook mushrooms without making them soggy?
faqs.answer: Don't wash — wipe with damp paper towel. Cook on medium-high heat with a thin layer of oil, don't crowd the pan, and don't add salt until the mushrooms are golden brown. Salt too early releases water and steams them instead of browning.

faqs.question: What's the best mushroom for scrambled eggs?
faqs.answer: Cremini (baby bella) mushrooms are the best all-around choice — meaty, earthy, and inexpensive. White button mushrooms work for milder flavor. For a restaurant feel, use shiitake or add truffle oil at the end of cooking.

faqs.question: Can I make scrambled eggs and mushrooms in advance?
faqs.answer: Scrambled eggs don't reheat well — they turn rubbery. Instead, pre-cook mushrooms on Sunday (keeps 3 days) and scramble fresh eggs each morning. This cuts the cook time to under 4 minutes. Alternatively, try [egg muffins](/blog/egg-muffins-meal-prep-recipe) — purpose-built for meal prep.
